problems.text:
Task
Below is a table of diameters of different denominations of United States
Coins:
###TABLE0###
If we place nickels around a central dime, as in the picture below, there is room
for five nickels with extra space but not enough room for a sixth nickel:
###IMAGE0###
How many dimes fit around a central dime?  What about around a
central nickel, quarter, or half dollar?  Do the dimes fit snugly or is there
extra space?
How many half dollars fit around a central dime?  What about around
a central nickel, quarter, or half dollar?  Do the half dollars fit snugly or
is there extra room?
Extending the work in (a) and (b) above, for positive numbers $r$ and $s$
how many circles of radius $s$ will fit around a central circle of radius $r$?

Task
Suppose $P$ is a point not contained on a line $L$.
Let $Q$ be the point on $L$ so that $PQ$ meets $L$ perpendicularly and let
$R$ be any other point on $L$ as in the picture below:
###IMAGE0###
Show that segment $PQ$ is shorter than segment $PR$.

metadata.title: Shortest line segment from a point $P$ to a line $L$

problems.text:
Problem 1
Brandon and Madison use different triangles to determine the slope of the line shown below.
###IMAGE0###
Brandon started at (0,-1) and drew a right triangle going up 2 units and right 3 units.
Madison started at (-3,-3) and drew a right triangle going up 6 units and right 9 units.
Draw and label both triangles on the graph.
How can Brandon and Madison use their triangles to find the slope?
What trigonometric ratio do Brandon and Madison use to find the slope?
Problem 2
Below is a line segment on a coordinate grid.
###IMAGE1###
Draw the right triangle that you can use to find the length of this line segment.
Find the length of the line segment.
Find the measure of all of the angles of the triangle formed.
Problem 3
Standing on the gallery of a lighthouse (the deck at the top of a lighthouse), a person spots a ship at an angle of depression of 20°. The lighthouse is 28 meters tall and sits on a cliff 45 meters tall as measured from sea level.  What is the horizontal distance between the lighthouse and the ship?

Samuel is at the top of a tower and will ride a trolley down a zip line to a lower tower. The total vertical drop of the zip line is 40 feet. The zip line’s angle of elevation from the lower tower is 11.5°. Sam’s friend is not going to zip-line but wants to walk along the ground from the tall tower to the lower tower. How far will Sam’s friend walk to meet him?

Task
The goal of this task is to estimate the measure of angles in triangles with integer side lengths.
What are the angle measures in a triangle whose three sides each have length of one unit?  Explain.
What are the approximate measures of the three angles in a triangle whose side lengths are 3, 4, and 5 units respectively? Explain.
What are the approximate angle measures of the three angles in a triangle whose side lengths are 2, 3, and 4 units respectively?

Problem 1
Watch the video
"Marine Ramp"
by Dan Meyer and determine which bridge is best.
Problem 2
Write a model that describes how to find the optimal ramp length, regardless of the horizontal and vertical displacement of the pier and the ramp.
Use the following link,
Boat Dock

Task
Selina has a 9 meter by 12 meter lawn that she wants to water. She has two sprinklers, each of which can water grass within an 8.4-meter radius. Selina wants to set up the two sprinklers so that they are on corners of the lawn. She would like for the sprinklers to water as much of the lawn as possible, because she will have to manually water the part of the lawn that is not covered by the sprinklers.
Selina considers two different strategies for placing the sprinklers. One strategy is to put the sprinklers at opposite ends of a 12-meter side of the lawn. The other is to put the sprinklers at opposite corners of the lawn. (See the figures below.) Which strategy appears to be best? Justify your answer.
###IMAGE0###
If Selina chooses the better of these two strategies, what percentage of the lawn will she be able to water with the sprinklers?
If Selina is allowed to put sprinklers in the interior of her lawn, how many sprinklers does she need to water the entire lawn?

Problem 1
Below is a right rectangular prism. The length of
$$\overline{AB}$$
is 4 inches and the length of
$$\overline{BC}$$
is 3 inches. The length of
$$\overline{MC}$$
is 8 inches. What is the volume of the right rectangular prism?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Robin made a model of a building. The model is composed of a right square pyramid glued exactly onto a right rectangular prism. The model and some of its dimensions are shown in the diagram below.
###IMAGE1###
What is the volume, in cubic inches, of the part of the model that is a right rectangular prism? Show or explain how you got your answer.
What is the height, in inches, of the part of the model that is a right square pyramid? Show or explain how you got your answer.
What is the volume, in cubic inches, of the entire model? Show or explain how you got your answer.

Task
Jerry and Ashley are trying to find out if it is possible to see the lowest point in the USA from the highest point in the USA. It turns out that the highest point in the United States, the peak of Mount Whitney, is only 85 miles from the lowest area, a large flat stretch of land in Death Valley called Badwater Basin.
Jerry thinks that the curvature of the earth will block the view of Badwater Basin from the peak of Mount Whitney. Ashley doesn't think that this is an issue and that they can neglect the curvature of the earth in their investigation.
As it turns out, it is not possible to see Badwater Basin from Mt. Whitney because the Panamint Range blocks the view. But what if there were no obstacles in the way? More precisely:
Standing on top of Mt. Whitney, is it possible to see a point on the surface of the earth 85 miles away, or would the curvature of the earth prevent this?

metadata.title: Neglecting the Curvature of the Earth

Alignments to Content Standards:  G-SRT.C.8
Task
The goal of this task is to estimate the measure of angles in triangles with integer side
lengths.
a. What are the angle measures in a triangle whose three sides each have length of one
unit?  Explain.
b. What are the approximate measures of the three angles in a triangle whose side
lengths are 3, 4, and 5 units respectively? Explain.
c. What are the approximate angle measures of the three angles in a triangle whose
side lengths are 2, 3, and 4 units respectively?
IM Commentary
The goal of this task is to estimate the measure of angles in triangles with integer side
lengths. In some cases, like the equilateral triangle, this can be done using properties of
the triangle. For right triangles, inverse trigonometric functions can be applied. In other
cases, like part (c), an auxiliary construction is needed before applying trigonometry to
calculate the angles. Since the task requires inverse trigonometric functions, students
will use technology to make appropriate estimates. One very interesting side note to
this task is that the algorithms that many calculators use in order to calculate complex
functions such as trigonometric functions use properties of trigonometric functions
along with some exact values for special angles like 30  and 45 .  
∘

Illustrative

Many ancient cultures reportedly used triangles with side lengths 3, 4, and 5 units in
order to construct right angles. This provided a simple means of making right angles
which would be very important in architecture and other design problems. More
information about how ancient cultures used Pythagorean triples in order to construct
right angles can be found in the commentary to this task:
https://www.illustrativemathematics.org/illustrations/724. One very interesting and
challenging extension question would be: what whole number side lengths are
necessary in order to construct angles within one half of a degree of each whole
number angle measure from 0 to 179? Or we could look for angles within any given
level of precision (one degree or one tenth of a degree, for example) of any given
angle.

Task
Jerry and Ashley are trying to find out if it is possible to see the lowest point in the USA
from the highest point in the USA. It turns out that the highest point in the United
States, the peak of Mount Whitney, is only 85 miles from the lowest area, a large flat
stretch of land in Death Valley called Badwater Basin.
Jerry thinks that the curvature of the earth will block the view of Badwater Basin from
the peak of Mount Whitney. Ashley doesn't think that this is an issue and that they can
neglect the curvature of the earth in their investigation.
As it turns out, it is not possible to see Badwater Basin from Mt. Whitney because the
Panamint Range blocks the view. But what if there were no obstacles in the way? More
precisely:
Standing on top of Mt. Whitney, is it possible to see a point on the surface of the earth 85
miles away, or would the curvature of the earth prevent this?

This is a foundational geometry task designed to provide a route for students to

Illustrative

develop some fundamental geometric properties that may seem rather obvious at first
glance. In this case, the fundamental property in question is that the shortest path
from a point to a line meets the line at a right angle, which is crucial for many further
developments in the subject. As such, teachers electing to have students justify this
result should make it clear which existing properties are available for use. In the
solution provided, a very brief proof is given which only relies on the Pythagorean
Theorem. It is probably worth remarking that the Pythagorean Theorem is the
cornerstone for evaluating the distance between two points, and here is used to
further solve the question of the distance between a point and a line.
This task can be used for instruction or for assessment. If it is used for instruction, the
teacher may want to have the students supply the picture. The task should be done
relatively early within a geometry course because of its intuitive appeal. In fact, an ideal
time to cover this task is at the same time that the notion of sraightline distance in the
plane is introduced. Since the result is so immediately plausible, it seems reasonable to
use this task for assessment purposes only in classes where providing proofs of such
statements is a regular occurrence.
Finally, we comment on alignment: The standard G-SRT.8 invokes using the
Pythagorean Theorem to solve applied problems. While the current task is not an
"applied problem" in the sense of physics and engineering, it gives a nice example of
applying one major result in mathematics in order to deduce further mathematics.

khan_exercises.title: Solve for a side in right triangles
khan_exercises.description: Given one side length and an acute angle in a right triangle, find another side using trigonometry.
topic_path: Solving for a side in a right triangle using the trigonometric ratios
Solve for a side in right triangles

items.question_content:
$AC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle B=40^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{7}$ into that equation.

$\sin(40^\circ)=\dfrac{AC}{7}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(40^\circ)&=\dfrac{AC}{7}\\\\\\
7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)&=AC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)\approx 4.5$

### Conclusion

$AC=4.5$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle A B C is forty degrees. Side A C is unknown. Side A B is seven units.

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=4\cdot\sin(70^\circ)\approx 3.76$

### Conclusion

$BC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle A=65^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{6}$ into that equation.

$\sin(65^\circ)=\dfrac{BC}{6}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(65^\circ)&=\dfrac{BC}{6}\\\\\\
6\cdot\sin(65^\circ)&=BC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$BC=6\cdot\sin(65^\circ)\approx 5.44$

### Conclusion

$BC=5.44$

### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle A=50^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{3}$ into that equation.

$\sin(50^\circ)=\dfrac{BC}{3}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(50^\circ)&=\dfrac{BC}{3}\\\\\\
3\cdot\sin(50^\circ)&=BC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$BC=3\cdot\sin(50^\circ)\approx 2.3$

### Conclusion

$BC=2.3$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle B A C is fifty degrees. Side B C is unknown. Side A B is three units.

items.question_content:
$BC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to the given angle, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to that angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{tangent}$.

2. Create an equation using the tangent of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The tangent of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side. This gives us the following equation.

$\tan(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\maroonC{AC}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle A=35^\circ$ and $\maroonC{AC}=\maroonC{2}$ into that equation.

$\tan(35^\circ)=\dfrac{BC}{2}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\tan(35^\circ)&=\dfrac{BC}{2}\\\\\\
2\cdot\tan(35^\circ)&=BC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$BC=2\cdot\tan(35^\circ)\approx 1.4$

### Conclusion

$BC=1.4$

khan_exercises.title: Solve for an angle in right triangles
khan_exercises.description: Given two sides in a right triangle, find one of the acute angles using trigonometry.
topic_path: Solving for an angle in a right triangle using the trigonometric ratios
Solve for an angle in right triangles

items.question_content:
$\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\sin^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{BC}=\blueD{2}$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{6}$ into that equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{2}{6}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(\angle A)&=\dfrac{2}{6}\\\\\\
\angle A&=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle A=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right) \approx 19.47^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle A=19.47^\circ$

$\begin{align}\tan(\angle A)&=\dfrac{9}{3}\\\\\\
\tan(\angle A)&=3\\\\
\angle A&=\tan^{-1}\left(3\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle A=\tan^{-1}\left(3\right) \approx 71.57^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle A=71.57^\circ$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle B A C is unknown. Side A C is three units. Side B C is nine units.

items.question_content:
$\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to that angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{tangent}$.

2. Create an equation using the tangent of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\tan^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The tangent of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side. This gives us the following equation.

$\tan(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\maroonC{AC}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{BC}=\blueD{5}$ and $\maroonC{AC}=\maroonC{7}$ into that equation.

$\tan(\angle A)=\dfrac{5}{7}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\tan(\angle A)&=\dfrac{5}{7}\\\\\\
\angle A&=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{7}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle A=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{7}\right) \approx 35.54^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle A=35.54^\circ$

khan_exercises.title: Pythagorean theorem challenge
khan_exercises.description: Solve real-world problems that can be modeled by right triangles, using the Pythagorean Theorem!

items.question_content:
Julian jogs $2$ kilometers east, $4$ kilometers north, and then $7$ kilometers west.  



[[☃ image 1]]

**How far is Julian from his starting position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers
hints:
We are looking for the length of $x$ in the picture below:



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

 In this case $a=4,b=5,$ and $c=x$.
To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, Julian is $6.4$ kilometers away from his starting position.

options.alt: A horizontal line segment with the endpoint on the left labeled start. It is two kilometers. The right endpoint is the endpoint of a vertical line segment that is four kilometers. The top endpoint of that line segment is also the endpoint of a horizontal line segment that is seven kilometers with the endpoint on the left labeled End. A dashed line connects from the point labeled start to the point labeled end.

items.question_content:
Carroll bikes $1$ kilometer east, $4$ kilometers north, and then $5$ kilometers east again.  

**How far is Carroll from her starting position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{km}$
hints:
The diagonal line is the $\blueD{\text{hypotenuse}}$ of a $\greenD{\text{right triangle}}$. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd589dc230028aef73185af1bf0156a1ed8b08cb)

We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

 In this case $a=6,b=4,$ and $ c=x$.
To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, Carroll is $7.2$ kilometers away from her starting position.

items.question_content:
A flagpole broke in a storm.  It was originally $81$ feet tall.  $28$ feet are still sticking straight out of the ground, where it snapped, but the remaining piece has hinged over and touches the ground some distance away.

**How far away is the end of the pole from the base of the pole along the ground?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] feet
hints:
Since the original height was $81$ feet, and $28 $ feet are still sticking straight out of the ground, the remaining piece must be $81-28=53$ feet long.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here $x$ is the distance we are interested in.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=x, b=28$, and $ c=53$.
The end of the pole is $45$ feet away from the base of the pole along the ground.

items.question_content:
A flagpole broke in a storm.  $7$ meters are still sticking straight out of the ground, where it snapped, but the remaining piece has hinged over and touches the ground at a point $24$ meters away horizontally.

**How tall was the flagpole before it broke?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
First, let's draw a picture.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here $x$ is the length of the piece of the flagpole that hinged over.

We're looking for the original length of the flagpole.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\text{total length}} &= \purpleD{\text{standing length}} + \blueE{\text{hinged length}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{7}+\blueE{x}
\end{align}$

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $\purpleD{a}^2 + \goldE{b}^2  = \blueE{c}^2$, where $\purpleD{a}$ and $\goldE{b}$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $\blueE{c}$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $\purpleD{a=7}$, $\goldE{b=24}$, and $\blueE{c=x}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\blueE{x}^2 &=\purpleD{7}^2+\goldE{24}^2\\\\
\blueE{x}^2 &=49+576\\\\
\blueE{x}^2 &= 625\\\\
\blueE{x} &= \sqrt{625}\\\\
\blueE{x}&= \blueE{25}
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
Ryan is putting a clothesline in his rectangular backyard.  He wants to put it between two trees on the edge of his property.  He has measured his property, and made the sketch shown below, where all the lengths listed are in meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the smallest length of rope that Ryan could buy which would stretch from one tree to the other?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
We need to find $x$:



[[☃ image 1]]

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=15, b=20$, and $ c=x$.
The smallest length of rope that would stretch from one tree to the other is $25$ meters.

options.alt: A rectangle with a width of twenty meters. On the left side of the rectangle, ten meters down is a point labeled Tree. Twenty-five down on the right side of the rectangle is another point labeled Tree. A slanted line connects both tree points.

items.question_content:
A monument in the shape of a right triangle sits on a rectangular pedestal that is $5$ meters high by $11$ meters long. The longest side of the triangular monument measures $61$ meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

**How high off the ground is the top of the monument?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.  The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=11, b=x$, and $ c=61$.

The height of the monument is $60$ meters. To find the height of the monument and pedestal together we can add.

$60 + 5 = 65$
The top of the monument is $65$ meters off the ground.

options.alt: A triangle and a rectangle share a side that is eleven units long. The triangle has a height of x units. The slanted side of the triangle is sixty-one units. The rectangle has the width of five units.

items.question_content:
An  airplane takes off, levels off, and lands according to the diagram shown below.
 
All measurements listed are in kilometers.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the horizontal distance from the plane's starting position to its finishing position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers
hints:
We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$ and $y$ in the picture below:



[[☃ image 1]]

Then the length we are looking for will be $x+3+y$.
The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.

Applying the theorem to the triangle on the left we have:

$
\begin{align}
x^2 +3^2&= 5^2\\\\
x^2 &= 5^2-3^2\\\\
x^2 &= 25-9\\\\
x^2 &=16\\\\
x&=4
\end{align}
$

Applying the theorem to the triangle on the right the we have:

$
\begin{align}
y^2 +3^2&= 7^2\\\\
y^2 &= 7^2-3^2\\\\
y^2 &= 49-9\\\\
y^2 &=40\\\\
y&=\sqrt{40}\\\\
y&\approx 6.3
\end{align}
$

So the distance we are looking for is $4 + 3+6.3 =13.3$

To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, the plane traveled $13.3$ kilometers horizontally.

options.alt: A square sharing a side with a right triangle on its left side and sharing a side with a right triangle on its right side. The square has side lengths of three kilometers. The right triangle on the left side has a base of x kilometers, a height of three kilometers, and a hypotenuse of five kilometers. The right triangle on the right side has a base of y kilometers, a height of three kilometers, and a hypotenuse of seven kilometers.

items.question_content:
The Pyraminx is a Rubik's cube-type toy  in the shape of a triangle-based pyramid.  Every edge of the Pyraminx below is  $15\,\text{cm}$ long. The pyramid's height is $h=12.2\,\text{cm}$.  The triangle drawn with dashed lines is a right triangle.

![Pyraminx toy](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f0e77b28f6dd464b73571abc733e634a.jpg)

![Triangle-based pyramid with height labeled "h" and radius labeled "r" from the bottom of the height segment to one vertex of the base of the pyramid. The height and radius form a right angle.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eed5cfc8bcb32e4aa9a76989bf6938b2376bdc13)

**What is the radius $r$ of the base of the pyramid?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

$r\approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. Every edge of the pyramid is $15\text{ cm}$ long.



[[☃ image 1]]

The radius $r$ is part of a right triangle.

Let's use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $r$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We have:

$\begin{align}
r^2+(12.2)^2&=(15)^2\\\\
r^2&=225-148.84\\\\
r&=\sqrt{76.16}\\\\
r&\approx8.7
\end{align}$

(rounded to the nearest tenth)
The radius of the base of the pyramid $r$ is approximately $8.7\,\text{cm}$.

items.question_content:
The Museu Blau in Barcelona, Spain, is a natural science museum built in the shape of a  triangular prism.  The base of the building is an equilateral triangle $180$ meters on each side.  The height of the prism is $25\,\text{m}$.


[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]]

**Find the height $h$ of the triangular base.**  
Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.

$h\approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
The base of the building is an equilateral triangle whose sides are each $180$ meters long.

The height $h$ of this triangle is part of a right triangle. We could find $h$ using the Pythagorean Theorem if we knew the length of the short leg in that right triangle.

How long is the short leg?
The height of the triangular base $h$ is approximately $155.9\,\text{m}$

options.alt: A museum in the shape of a triangular prism.
A triangle with a base of one hundred eighty meters. Its height is h meters. Its other sides are one hundred eighty meters.

items.question_content:
The Tetrahedron [[☃ definition 2]] designed by Jonathan Schwinge is a [[☃ definition 1]] with edges $20\,\text{m}$  long. The distance $r$ from one corner to the center of the base (shown in the picture below) is $11.5\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the vertical height $h$ of the tetrahedron?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.*

$h \approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. Every edge of the tetrahedron is $20\text{ m}$ long.



[[☃ image 1]]

The height $h$ is part of a right triangle.

Let's use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $h$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We have:

$\begin{align}h^2+(11.5)^2&=(20)^2\\\\
h^2&=400-132.25\\\\
&=267.75\\\\
 h&=\sqrt{267.75}\\\\
&\approx16.4\end{align}$

(rounded to the nearest tenth)
The vertical height of the tetrahedron $h$ is approximately $16.4\,\text{m}$.

options.definition: A triangular prism where every edge is the same length
A large boat for cruising or racing

options.alt: A yacht in the shape of a regular tetrahedron. The distance from the corner to the center of the base r meters long and a vertical height of h meters.

items.question_content:
The Palace of Peace and Accord in Astana, Kazakhstan, was built in $2006$.  The building has a right square-based pyramid structure. The length of a side of the square base is $62\,\text{m}$, and the vertical height of the pyramid is also $62\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]]

**What is the slant height $\ell$ of one of the triangular faces?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.*

$\ell \approx$  [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. The length of a side of the square base is $62\,\text{m}$, and the vertical height of the pyramid is also $62\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The slant height of the face of the pyramid is along the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs of $62\text{ m}$ and $31\text{ m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We find its height using the Pythagorean Theorem:

$\begin{align}
\blueE{\ell}^2&=(31)^2+(\maroonD{62})^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell}^2&=(31)^2+2^2\cdot(31)^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell}^2&=5(31)^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell} &=31\sqrt5 \\\\
&\approx69.3
\end{align}$

(Rounded to the nearest tenth)
The slant height of one of the triangular faces $\ell$ is approximately $69.3\,\text{m}$.

options.alt: A building in the shape of a square pyramid.
A square pyramid with a slant height l units long.

items.question_content:
A builder makes all of their ramps with a base to height ratio of $12:1$ to be wheelchair-accessible. See the diagram below, which is not drawn to scale:



[[☃ image 1]]

A certain ramp needs to cover a height of $0.8$ meters.



[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of this ramp?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth of a meter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given a base to height ratio of $12:1$, how long is the hypotenuse $\maroonD{c}$ in this ratio?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $\maroonD{c}$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can use similar triangles to solve for the ramp length $\maroonD{\ell}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Solve for $\maroonD{c}$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
1^2+12^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
145 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
\maroonD{c} &= \sqrt{145}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the ramp $\ell$ is approximately $9.63\,\text{m}$.

options.alt: A right triangle with its short leg dashed and one meter. Its long leg is dashed and twelve meters.
A right triangle with its short leg dashed and point eight meter. Its long leg is dashed. The hypotenuse is l meters.

items.question_content:
A builder makes staircases where each step is $28\,\text{cm}$ long and rises $18\,\text{cm}$ so people don't trip. Every staircase has a stringer — a diagonal support connecting all of the steps.



[[☃ image 2]]

A certain staircase will have $6$ steps.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of the stringer for this staircase?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given that each step is $28\,\text{cm}$ long and rises $18\,\text{cm}$, how long is the hypotenuse $c$ in each step?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $c$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can find the length $\ell$ of the stringer.

### Solve for $c$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= c^2
\\\\
18^2+28^2 &= c^2
\\\\
1108 &= c^2
\\\\
c&= \sqrt{1108}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the stringer $\ell$ is approximately $199.7\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A staircase of six stairs. The distance from the bottom of the first step to the top of the sixth step is labeled L.
A right triangle with a dashed hypotenuse labeled stringer. The short leg is eighteen centimeters and a long leg of twenty-eight centimeters.

items.question_content:
A builder makes drainpipes that drop $1\,\text{cm}$ over a horizontal distance of $30\,\text{cm}$ to prevent clogs. See the diagram below, which is not drawn to scale:



[[☃ image 1]]

A certain drainpipe needs to cover a horizontal distance of $700\,\text{cm}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of this drainpipe?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given a ratio of $1:30$, how long is the hypotenuse $\maroonD{c}$ in this ratio?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $\maroonD{c}$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can use similar triangles to solve for the drainpipe length $\maroonD{\ell}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Solve for $\maroonD{c}$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
1^2+30^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
901 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
\maroonD{c} &= \sqrt{901}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the drainpipe $\ell$ is approximately $700.4\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A right triangle with its short leg dashed and one centimeter. Its long leg is dashed and thirty centimeters.
A right triangle with Its long leg  dashed  and seven hundred centimeters long. The hypotenuse is l centimeters.

khan_exercises.title: Solve for an angle in right triangles
khan_exercises.description: Given two sides in a right triangle, find one of the acute angles using trigonometry.
topic_path: Solving for an angle in a right triangle using the trigonometric ratios
Solve for an angle in right triangles

items.question_content:
$\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\sin^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{BC}=\blueD{2}$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{6}$ into that equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{2}{6}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(\angle A)&=\dfrac{2}{6}\\\\\\
\angle A&=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle A=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right) \approx 19.47^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle A=19.47^\circ$

items.question_content:
$\angle B=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\sin^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{AC}=\blueD{7}$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{9}$ into that equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{7}{9}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(\angle B)&=\dfrac{7}{9}\\\\\\
\angle B&=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{7}{9}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle B=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{7}{9}\right) \approx 51.06^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle B=51.06^\circ$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle A B C is unknown. Side A C is seven units. Side A B is nine units.

items.question_content:
$\angle B=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to that angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{tangent}$.

2. Create an equation using the tangent of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\tan^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The tangent of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side. This gives us the following equation.

$\tan(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\maroonC{BC}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{AC}=\blueD{4}$ and $\maroonC{BC}=\maroonC{3}$ into that equation.

$\tan(\angle B)=\dfrac{4}{3}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\tan(\angle B)&=\dfrac{4}{3}\\\\\\
\angle B&=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle B=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle B=53.13^\circ$

items.question_content:
$\angle B=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to that angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{tangent}$.

2. Create an equation using the tangent of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\tan^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The tangent of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side. This gives us the following equation.

$\tan(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\maroonC{BC}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{AC}=\blueD{5}$ and $\maroonC{BC}=\maroonC{6}$ into that equation.

$\tan(\angle B)=\dfrac{5}{6}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\tan(\angle B)&=\dfrac{5}{6}\\\\\\
\angle B&=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{6}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle B=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{6}\right) \approx 39.81^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle B=39.81^\circ$

items.question_content:
$AC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle B=40^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{7}$ into that equation.

$\sin(40^\circ)=\dfrac{AC}{7}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(40^\circ)&=\dfrac{AC}{7}\\\\\\
7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)&=AC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)\approx 4.5$

### Conclusion

$AC=4.5$

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{cosine}$.

2. Create an equation using the cosine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The cosine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\cos(\angle A)=\dfrac{\maroonC{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle A=65^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{7}$ into that equation.

$\cos(65^\circ)=\dfrac{AC}{7}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\cos(65^\circ)&=\dfrac{AC}{7}\\\\\\
7\cdot\cos(65^\circ)&=AC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=7\cdot\cos(65^\circ)\approx 2.96$

### Conclusion

$AC=2.96$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle B A C is sixty-five degrees. Side A C is unknown. Side A B is seven units.

BC\cdot\tan(20^\circ)&=4\\\\
BC&=\dfrac{4}{\tan(20^\circ)}
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$BC=\dfrac{4}{\tan(20^\circ)}\approx 10.99$

### Conclusion

$BC=10.99$

options.alt: A right triangle A B C. Angle A C B is a right angle. Angle A B C is twenty degrees. Side B C is unknown. Side A C is four units.

khan_exercises.title: Pythagorean theorem challenge
khan_exercises.description: Solve real-world problems that can be modeled by right triangles, using the Pythagorean Theorem!

items.question_content:
Julian jogs $2$ kilometers east, $4$ kilometers north, and then $7$ kilometers west.  



[[☃ image 1]]

**How far is Julian from his starting position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers
hints:
We are looking for the length of $x$ in the picture below:



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

 In this case $a=4,b=5,$ and $c=x$.
To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, Julian is $6.4$ kilometers away from his starting position.

options.alt: A horizontal line segment with the endpoint on the left labeled start. It is two kilometers. The right endpoint is the endpoint of a vertical line segment that is four kilometers. The top endpoint of that line segment is also the endpoint of a horizontal line segment that is seven kilometers with the endpoint on the left labeled End. A dashed line connects from the point labeled start to the point labeled end.

items.question_content:
Carroll bikes $1$ kilometer east, $4$ kilometers north, and then $5$ kilometers east again.  

**How far is Carroll from her starting position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{km}$
hints:
The diagonal line is the $\blueD{\text{hypotenuse}}$ of a $\greenD{\text{right triangle}}$. 

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd589dc230028aef73185af1bf0156a1ed8b08cb)

We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

 In this case $a=6,b=4,$ and $ c=x$.
To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, Carroll is $7.2$ kilometers away from her starting position.

items.question_content:
A flagpole broke in a storm.  It was originally $81$ feet tall.  $28$ feet are still sticking straight out of the ground, where it snapped, but the remaining piece has hinged over and touches the ground some distance away.

**How far away is the end of the pole from the base of the pole along the ground?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] feet
hints:
Since the original height was $81$ feet, and $28 $ feet are still sticking straight out of the ground, the remaining piece must be $81-28=53$ feet long.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here $x$ is the distance we are interested in.

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=x, b=28$, and $ c=53$.
The end of the pole is $45$ feet away from the base of the pole along the ground.

items.question_content:
A flagpole broke in a storm.  $7$ meters are still sticking straight out of the ground, where it snapped, but the remaining piece has hinged over and touches the ground at a point $24$ meters away horizontally.

**How tall was the flagpole before it broke?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
First, let's draw a picture.



[[☃ image 1]]

Here $x$ is the length of the piece of the flagpole that hinged over.

We're looking for the original length of the flagpole.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\text{total length}} &= \purpleD{\text{standing length}} + \blueE{\text{hinged length}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{7}+\blueE{x}
\end{align}$

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $\purpleD{a}^2 + \goldE{b}^2  = \blueE{c}^2$, where $\purpleD{a}$ and $\goldE{b}$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $\blueE{c}$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $\purpleD{a=7}$, $\goldE{b=24}$, and $\blueE{c=x}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\blueE{x}^2 &=\purpleD{7}^2+\goldE{24}^2\\\\
\blueE{x}^2 &=49+576\\\\
\blueE{x}^2 &= 625\\\\
\blueE{x} &= \sqrt{625}\\\\
\blueE{x}&= \blueE{25}
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
Ryan is putting a clothesline in his rectangular backyard.  He wants to put it between two trees on the edge of his property.  He has measured his property, and made the sketch shown below, where all the lengths listed are in meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the smallest length of rope that Ryan could buy which would stretch from one tree to the other?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
We need to find $x$:



[[☃ image 1]]

The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=15, b=20$, and $ c=x$.
The smallest length of rope that would stretch from one tree to the other is $25$ meters.

options.alt: A rectangle with a width of twenty meters. On the left side of the rectangle, ten meters down is a point labeled Tree. Twenty-five down on the right side of the rectangle is another point labeled Tree. A slanted line connects both tree points.

items.question_content:
A monument in the shape of a right triangle sits on a rectangular pedestal that is $5$ meters high by $11$ meters long. The longest side of the triangular monument measures $61$ meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

**How high off the ground is the top of the monument?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$.  The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.  

In this case $a=11, b=x$, and $ c=61$.

The height of the monument is $60$ meters. To find the height of the monument and pedestal together we can add.

$60 + 5 = 65$
The top of the monument is $65$ meters off the ground.

options.alt: A triangle and a rectangle share a side that is eleven units long. The triangle has a height of x units. The slanted side of the triangle is sixty-one units. The rectangle has the width of five units.

items.question_content:
An  airplane takes off, levels off, and lands according to the diagram shown below.
 
All measurements listed are in kilometers.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the horizontal distance from the plane's starting position to its finishing position?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a kilometer.*

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilometers
hints:
We can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $x$ and $y$ in the picture below:



[[☃ image 1]]

Then the length we are looking for will be $x+3+y$.
The equation for the Pythagorean Theorem is $a^2 + b^2  = c^2$, where $a$ and $b$ are the lengths of the two legs of the triangle, and $c$ is the length of the hypotenuse.

Applying the theorem to the triangle on the left we have:

$
\begin{align}
x^2 +3^2&= 5^2\\\\
x^2 &= 5^2-3^2\\\\
x^2 &= 25-9\\\\
x^2 &=16\\\\
x&=4
\end{align}
$

Applying the theorem to the triangle on the right the we have:

$
\begin{align}
y^2 +3^2&= 7^2\\\\
y^2 &= 7^2-3^2\\\\
y^2 &= 49-9\\\\
y^2 &=40\\\\
y&=\sqrt{40}\\\\
y&\approx 6.3
\end{align}
$

So the distance we are looking for is $4 + 3+6.3 =13.3$

To the nearest tenth of a kilometer, the plane traveled $13.3$ kilometers horizontally.

options.alt: A square sharing a side with a right triangle on its left side and sharing a side with a right triangle on its right side. The square has side lengths of three kilometers. The right triangle on the left side has a base of x kilometers, a height of three kilometers, and a hypotenuse of five kilometers. The right triangle on the right side has a base of y kilometers, a height of three kilometers, and a hypotenuse of seven kilometers.

items.question_content:
The Pyraminx is a Rubik's cube-type toy  in the shape of a triangle-based pyramid.  Every edge of the Pyraminx below is  $15\,\text{cm}$ long. The pyramid's height is $h=12.2\,\text{cm}$.  The triangle drawn with dashed lines is a right triangle.

![Pyraminx toy](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f0e77b28f6dd464b73571abc733e634a.jpg)

![Triangle-based pyramid with height labeled "h" and radius labeled "r" from the bottom of the height segment to one vertex of the base of the pyramid. The height and radius form a right angle.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eed5cfc8bcb32e4aa9a76989bf6938b2376bdc13)

**What is the radius $r$ of the base of the pyramid?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

$r\approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. Every edge of the pyramid is $15\text{ cm}$ long.



[[☃ image 1]]

The radius $r$ is part of a right triangle.

Let's use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $r$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We have:

$\begin{align}
r^2+(12.2)^2&=(15)^2\\\\
r^2&=225-148.84\\\\
r&=\sqrt{76.16}\\\\
r&\approx8.7
\end{align}$

(rounded to the nearest tenth)
The radius of the base of the pyramid $r$ is approximately $8.7\,\text{cm}$.

items.question_content:
The Museu Blau in Barcelona, Spain, is a natural science museum built in the shape of a  triangular prism.  The base of the building is an equilateral triangle $180$ meters on each side.  The height of the prism is $25\,\text{m}$.


[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]]

**Find the height $h$ of the triangular base.**  
Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.

$h\approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
The base of the building is an equilateral triangle whose sides are each $180$ meters long.

The height $h$ of this triangle is part of a right triangle. We could find $h$ using the Pythagorean Theorem if we knew the length of the short leg in that right triangle.

How long is the short leg?
The height of the triangular base $h$ is approximately $155.9\,\text{m}$

options.alt: A museum in the shape of a triangular prism.
A triangle with a base of one hundred eighty meters. Its height is h meters. Its other sides are one hundred eighty meters.

items.question_content:
The Tetrahedron [[☃ definition 2]] designed by Jonathan Schwinge is a [[☃ definition 1]] with edges $20\,\text{m}$  long. The distance $r$ from one corner to the center of the base (shown in the picture below) is $11.5\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the vertical height $h$ of the tetrahedron?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.*

$h \approx$ [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. Every edge of the tetrahedron is $20\text{ m}$ long.



[[☃ image 1]]

The height $h$ is part of a right triangle.

Let's use the Pythagorean Theorem to find $h$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We have:

$\begin{align}h^2+(11.5)^2&=(20)^2\\\\
h^2&=400-132.25\\\\
&=267.75\\\\
 h&=\sqrt{267.75}\\\\
&\approx16.4\end{align}$

(rounded to the nearest tenth)
The vertical height of the tetrahedron $h$ is approximately $16.4\,\text{m}$.

options.definition: A triangular prism where every edge is the same length
A large boat for cruising or racing

options.alt: A yacht in the shape of a regular tetrahedron. The distance from the corner to the center of the base r meters long and a vertical height of h meters.

items.question_content:
The Palace of Peace and Accord in Astana, Kazakhstan, was built in $2006$.  The building has a right square-based pyramid structure. The length of a side of the square base is $62\,\text{m}$, and the vertical height of the pyramid is also $62\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]]

**What is the slant height $\ell$ of one of the triangular faces?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a meter.*

$\ell \approx$  [[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
First, let's label our given information. The length of a side of the square base is $62\,\text{m}$, and the vertical height of the pyramid is also $62\,\text{m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The slant height of the face of the pyramid is along the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs of $62\text{ m}$ and $31\text{ m}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We find its height using the Pythagorean Theorem:

$\begin{align}
\blueE{\ell}^2&=(31)^2+(\maroonD{62})^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell}^2&=(31)^2+2^2\cdot(31)^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell}^2&=5(31)^2\\\\
\blueE{\ell} &=31\sqrt5 \\\\
&\approx69.3
\end{align}$

(Rounded to the nearest tenth)
The slant height of one of the triangular faces $\ell$ is approximately $69.3\,\text{m}$.

options.alt: A building in the shape of a square pyramid.
A square pyramid with a slant height l units long.

items.question_content:
A builder makes all of their ramps with a base to height ratio of $12:1$ to be wheelchair-accessible. See the diagram below, which is not drawn to scale:



[[☃ image 1]]

A certain ramp needs to cover a height of $0.8$ meters.



[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of this ramp?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth of a meter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given a base to height ratio of $12:1$, how long is the hypotenuse $\maroonD{c}$ in this ratio?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $\maroonD{c}$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can use similar triangles to solve for the ramp length $\maroonD{\ell}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Solve for $\maroonD{c}$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
1^2+12^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
145 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
\maroonD{c} &= \sqrt{145}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the ramp $\ell$ is approximately $9.63\,\text{m}$.

options.alt: A right triangle with its short leg dashed and one meter. Its long leg is dashed and twelve meters.
A right triangle with its short leg dashed and point eight meter. Its long leg is dashed. The hypotenuse is l meters.

items.question_content:
A builder makes staircases where each step is $28\,\text{cm}$ long and rises $18\,\text{cm}$ so people don't trip. Every staircase has a stringer — a diagonal support connecting all of the steps.



[[☃ image 2]]

A certain staircase will have $6$ steps.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of the stringer for this staircase?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given that each step is $28\,\text{cm}$ long and rises $18\,\text{cm}$, how long is the hypotenuse $c$ in each step?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $c$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can find the length $\ell$ of the stringer.

### Solve for $c$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= c^2
\\\\
18^2+28^2 &= c^2
\\\\
1108 &= c^2
\\\\
c&= \sqrt{1108}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the stringer $\ell$ is approximately $199.7\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A staircase of six stairs. The distance from the bottom of the first step to the top of the sixth step is labeled L.
A right triangle with a dashed hypotenuse labeled stringer. The short leg is eighteen centimeters and a long leg of twenty-eight centimeters.

items.question_content:
A builder makes drainpipes that drop $1\,\text{cm}$ over a horizontal distance of $30\,\text{cm}$ to prevent clogs. See the diagram below, which is not drawn to scale:



[[☃ image 1]]

A certain drainpipe needs to cover a horizontal distance of $700\,\text{cm}$.



[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the length $\ell$ of this drainpipe?**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth of a centimeter.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

Given a ratio of $1:30$, how long is the hypotenuse $\maroonD{c}$ in this ratio?



[[☃ image 1]]

First, let's solve for $\maroonD{c}$ using the Pythagorean theorem.

Then, we can use similar triangles to solve for the drainpipe length $\maroonD{\ell}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

### Solve for $\maroonD{c}$

$\begin{align}
a^2+b^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
1^2+30^2 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
901 &= \maroonD{c}^2
\\\\
\maroonD{c} &= \sqrt{901}
\end{align}$

(Let's not round until the end.)

### Answer

The length of the drainpipe $\ell$ is approximately $700.4\,\text{cm}$.

A right triangle with Its long leg  dashed  and seven hundred centimeters long. The hypotenuse is l centimeters.

khan_exercises.title: Solve for a side in right triangles
khan_exercises.description: Given one side length and an acute angle in a right triangle, find another side using trigonometry.
topic_path: Solving for sides and angles in right triangles using the trigonometric ratios
Solve for a side in right triangles

items.question_content:
$AC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$, and we are asked to find the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle B=40^\circ$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{7}$ into that equation.

$\sin(40^\circ)=\dfrac{AC}{7}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(40^\circ)&=\dfrac{AC}{7}\\\\\\
7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)&=AC
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=7\cdot\sin(40^\circ)\approx 4.5$

### Conclusion

$AC=4.5$

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle B)=\dfrac{\blueD{AC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle B=65^\circ$ and $\blueD{AC}=\blueD{5}$ into that equation.

$\sin(65^\circ)=\dfrac{5}{AB}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(65^\circ)&=\dfrac{5}{AB}\\\\\\
AB\cdot\sin(65^\circ)&=5\\\\
AB&=\dfrac{5}{\sin(65^\circ)}
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AB=\dfrac{5}{\sin(65^\circ)}\approx 5.52$

### Conclusion

$AB=5.52$

items.question_content:
$AC=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the given angle, and we are asked to find the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to that angle. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{tangent}$.

2. Create an equation using the tangent of the given angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing side and evaluate using a calculator.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The tangent of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side. This gives us the following equation.

$\tan(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\maroonC{AC}}$

Now we can substitute $\angle A=70^\circ$ and $\blueD{BC}=\blueD{6}$ into that equation.

$\tan(70^\circ)=\dfrac{6}{AC}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\tan(70^\circ)&=\dfrac{6}{AC}\\\\\\
AC\cdot\tan(70^\circ)&=6\\\\
AC&=\dfrac{6}{\tan(70^\circ)}
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$AC=\dfrac{6}{\tan(70^\circ)}\approx 2.18$

### Conclusion

$AC=2.18$

topic_path: Solving for an angle in a right triangle using the trigonometric ratios
Solve for an angle in right triangles

items.question_content:
$\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{sine}$.

2. Create an equation using the sine of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\sin^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The sine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\blueD{\text{opposite}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{\blueD{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\blueD{BC}=\blueD{2}$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{6}$ into that equation.

$\sin(\angle A)=\dfrac{2}{6}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\sin(\angle A)&=\dfrac{2}{6}\\\\\\
\angle A&=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle A=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right) \approx 19.47^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle A=19.47^\circ$

items.question_content:
$\angle B=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$^\circ$

*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*


[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

1. Note that we are given the length of the side $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ to the missing angle, and the length of the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. The trigonometric ratio that contains both of those sides is the $\Large\text{cosine}$.

2. Create an equation using the cosine of the missing angle.

3. Solve the equation for the missing angle using the $\cos^{-1}$ function.

### Creating the corresponding equation

The cosine of an angle is the ratio of the angle's $\maroonC{\text{adjacent}}$ side to the $\purpleC{\text{hypotenuse}}$. This gives us the following equation.

$\cos(\angle B)=\dfrac{\maroonC{BC}}{\purpleC{AB}}$

Now we can substitute $\maroonC{BC}=\maroonC{4}$ and $\purpleC{AB}=\purpleC{5}$ into that equation.

$\cos(\angle B)=\dfrac{4}{5}$



[[☃ image 1]]

### Solving the equation

$\begin{align}\cos(\angle B)&=\dfrac{4}{5}\\\\\\
\angle B&=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle B=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right) \approx 36.87^\circ$

### Conclusion

$\angle B=36.87^\circ$

\angle B&=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)
\end{align}$

Now let's evaluate this result in the calculator and round to the nearest hundredth.

$\angle B=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^\circ$

### Conclusion
